title: Where Did 'Fast' and 'Now' Come From?
subtitle: A reading on the cultural roots of the impatient relationship with the body
excerpt: A reading on where the demand for fast results and immediate transformation came from in women's relationship with their bodies. Examines the commercial and cultural forces behind the urgency, and what a different timeline might feel like.
sourceNotes: Written for homemade.education. It draws on cultural writing about diet culture, body image, and the way women's self-improvement got sold back to them.

content.text: A reading on where the urgency came from. The expectation that body change should happen fast, that results should be visible quickly, that patience is failure: these are not neutral observations about how bodies work. They are a set of ideas with a specific commercial history. This reading looks at where they came from and what it costs to live inside them.

The diet and wellness industry runs on before-and-after. The before is always urgent: something is wrong, it needs fixing, time is being lost. The after is always close: a few weeks, a programme, a system. The timeline between them is almost always compressed into something biologically unrealistic, covering fat loss, fitness, skin, and energy. The gap between what the body can actually do and what the culture promises it should do is where most of the self-blame lives.

The fast timeline did not come from physiology. It came from marketing. A twelve-week programme is not twelve weeks because that is how long human biology needs. It is twelve weeks because that is a sellable unit. The urgency is a design feature of the industry, not a fact about the body.

The demand for quick results creates a specific kind of relationship with the body: the body is a project that should be completing itself on schedule. When it doesn't, the conclusion is usually that something is wrong with the person doing the project, not that the timeline was arbitrary. This means the body is regularly failing a test that was designed to produce failure.

The emotional cost is the deferred life: the holiday not taken, the photo not wanted, the thing held in reserve for when the body is different. The now demand means the actual present body is always insufficient, always waiting room, always not yet.
What a different timeline looks like

Bodies change slowly. Consistently applied small inputs produce results across months and years, not days and weeks. The research on sustainable change, in fitness, in weight, in sleep, and in stress, consistently points to slow and steady as the only thing that lasts. This is not a consolation prize. It is the actual biology.

A longer timeline also means the present body is allowed to exist without probation. The body today does not need to be improved before it counts. The life lived in a body that hasn't reached the target is still a life. The urgency that says otherwise is borrowed from an industry that profits from your impatience.
Written for homemade.education. The piece draws on cultural writing about diet culture and body image. It draws on writing about how women's self-improvement got sold back to them.
